answer:
**Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1.**

This text pairs a doctrine of Dharma's progressive decline across the four yugas—with all dharmas arising fully in the Kṛta and being lost by the Kali—with age-specific supreme or "highest" dharmas: austerity (*tapas*) in the Kṛta, knowledge (*jñāna*) in the Tretā, sacrifice (*yajña*) in the Dvāpara, and giving (*dāna*) alone in the Kali.[^1][^2][^3]

The key verses are Par_1.16 (noting Dharma's full emergence and eventual loss), Par_1.22 (dharmas vary by yuga), and especially Par_1.23 (explicitly naming the highest dharma per age). Par_1.24 adds that Parāśara's own teachings apply to the Kali.[^4]

[^1]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-d9b4b0](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-d9b4b0). "In this Manvantara, there are *dharmas* for the Kṛta, Tretā, and other *yugas*. All *dharmas* arose in the Kṛta *yuga*; all are lost in the Kali *yuga*."

[^2]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-11656a](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-11656a). "The *dharmas* for men are different in the Kṛta *yuga*, the Tretā, and the Dvāpara *yuga*. They are different again in the Kali *yuga*, in accordance with the character of the age."

[^3]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-afd7d9](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-afd7d9). "Austerity (*tapas*) is supreme in the Kṛta *yuga*; knowledge (*jñāna*) is said to be so in the Tretā. In the Dvāpara, they call it sacrifice (*yajña*); in the Kali *yuga*, only charity (*dāna*) is supreme."

[^4]: Parāśara Smṛti, Chapter 1, [p-121575](/works/unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be/01/translation/#p-121575). "In the Kṛta *yuga*, the *dharmas* are those of Manu; in the Tretā, those of Gautama are remembered. In the Dvāpara, they are those of Śaṅkha and Likhita; in the Kali *yuga*, those of Parāśara are remembered."
